message:
feat(ftui): migrate Events view with filtering and 60/40 detail panel (FTUI-05.4)

Implements the Events view for the FrankenTUI backend with full parity
to the legacy ratatui Events view:

- EventsViewState with filtered_indices() and clamped_selection()
- handle_events_key: j/k/Up/Down nav, u=unhandled toggle, 0-9=pane
  filter, Backspace=delete char, Esc=clear
- render_events_view: 60/40 split layout (event list + detail panel)
  with column headers, severity indicators, handled markers
- Digit key routing fix: '1'-'7' forward to view handler in Events
  view instead of triggering global view switch
- Events data refresh in refresh_data() via QueryClient.list_events()
- 17 tests covering filtering, navigation, key handling, rendering

Also includes collaborative changes from LavenderGrove: Search view
(FTUI-05.7) and Help view (FTUI-05.8) with rendering + key handling.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"wa-1wg","title":"[EPIC] Backup & Restore: Database Export, Import, Scheduled Backups","description":"# [EPIC] Backup & Restore System

## Mission
Provide users with confidence that their terminal history and configuration will never be lost, and make migration between machines or versions painless.

## Why This Matters
wa accumulates valuable data:
- Terminal transcripts (searchable history)
- Event timeline (debugging context)
- Configuration (tuned patterns, workflows)

Loss of this data would be a significant trust violation. Users need:
- Peace of mind that data is safe
- Easy migration to new machines
- Recovery from corruption or mistakes

## Components

### 1. Database Export (`wa backup export`)
Export the entire database to a portable format:
```bash
$ wa backup export --output backup_2026-01-18.wa
Creating backup...
  Segments: 12,345
  Events: 567
  Patterns: 23
  Config: included
Backup saved: backup_2026-01-18.wa (45 MB)
SHA256: abc123...
```

Export format:
- Self-describing archive (version header + manifest)
- SQLite dump + JSON metadata
- Optional compression (zstd)
- Integrity checksum

### 2. Database Import (`wa backup import`)
Restore from a backup:
```bash
$ wa backup import backup_2026-01-18.wa --verify
Verifying backup integrity... OK
Backup version: 1.2.0 (compatible)
Contents:
  Segments: 12,345
  Events: 567
  Config: included

Restore options:
  [1] Full restore (replace current data)
  [2] Merge (keep newer, import older)
  [3] Dry-run (show what would change)
```

Safety:
- Require explicit confirmation for full restore
- Create automatic backup before import
- Validate schema compatibility

### 3. Incremental Backups
For users who want continuous protection:
```toml
[backup]
enabled = true
schedule = "daily"  # or "hourly", "weekly"
retention_days = 30
destination = "~/.local/share/wa/backups/"
max_backups = 10
```

### 4. Configuration Export/Import
Separate from data, for sharing configs:
```bash
$ wa config export --output my-config.toml
$ wa config import my-config.toml --merge
```

### 5. Migration Assistant
When upgrading wa with breaking changes:
```bash
$ wa upgrade --from 1.x
Detected wa 1.x database
Migration required for:
  - Schema v1 → v2 (new event columns)
  - Config format update

Proceed? [y/N]
```

## Success Criteria
A user should be able to:
- create a backup file that is self-contained and verifiable (manifest + checksums)
- restore safely with a preview/dry-run path before any destructive change
- migrate between machines without manual file spelunking

Operational expectations (v0):
- backups are deterministic and explainable (stable manifest, clear versions)
- backups do not leak secrets (config redaction + no raw transcript dumping)
- imports refuse unsafe/incompatible inputs with actionable errors
- scheduled backups are robust (bounded disk usage, retention policy enforced)

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - Export/import round-trip preserves all data
  - Version detection works across schema versions
  - Merge logic handles conflicts correctly

- Integration tests:
  - Backup with active watcher (snapshot isolation)
  - Import into empty vs populated database
  - Scheduled backup runs correctly

- E2E tests:
  - Full backup/restore cycle with real data
  - Migration from mock old version
  - Corrupt backup rejected with clear error

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] wa backup export creates portable backup files
- [ ] wa backup import restores with verification
- [ ] Incremental backup scheduling works
- [ ] wa config export/import for config-only migration
- [ ] Version migration assistant for upgrades
- [ ] All backups include integrity checksums
- [ ] Clear error messages for incompatible backups","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:55:22.915697374Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-29T02:58:36.963666064Z","closed_at":"2026-01-29T02:58:36.963516365Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-reliability","area-ux","phase-4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1wg","depends_on_id":"wa-37x","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:24Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-20gu","title":"FTUI-05.2.a Home view parity checklist and intentional delta ledger","description":"## Background
Home view migration needs explicit parity tracking to avoid silent UX regressions during frankentui adoption.

## Deliverables
- widget-by-widget parity checklist (layout, indicators, actions, refresh behavior)
- intentional-delta ledger with justification and user-impact notes
- evidence links to snapshots and test outputs for each checklist item

## Acceptance Criteria
- every Home widget state has either parity confirmation or approved intentional delta
- checklist references deterministic snapshot and unit-test artifacts
- failure notes include reproducible steps and relevant logs for debugging.,","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:14:16.495001658Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-08T21:30:38.890049283Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ftui","subtask","views"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-20gu","depends_on_id":"wa-2i6m","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:24:59.743651770Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-20gu","depends_on_id":"wa-3pc9","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:14:16.516338961Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"}]}

 {"id":"wa-24l8","title":"[EPIC] FTUI-07 Test, Quality, and CI Gate Migration","description":"## Purpose
Establish migration-specific test gates, artifact standards, and CI enforcement.

## Why
A UI migration without strict quality gates will drift and become impossible to trust.

## Focus
- unit and snapshot coverage for ftui views
- PTY E2E for lifecycle/input/resize/log stress
- perf budgets and regression gates
- docs-smoke and schema-level drift checks","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:07:23.549109561Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-08T20:26:28.689942124Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ftui","migration","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-24l8","depends_on_id":"wa-2wed","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:07:23.568122609Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-24l8","depends_on_id":"wa-2zd7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:15:10.578633853Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-24l8","depends_on_id":"wa-38vw","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:15:06.713124158Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"}],"comments":[{"id":274,"issue_id":"wa-24l8","author":"GrayHarbor","text":"### Execution Rationale (FTUI-07)
FTUI-07 converts quality expectations into mandatory gates so migration quality is enforceable, not aspirational.","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:26:28Z"}]}
 {"id":"wa-290k","title":"FTUI-08.1 Capture pre/post migration performance baselines","description":"## Background\nWe need objective evidence that migration does not degrade operator responsiveness.\n\n## Deliverables\n- baseline metrics from ratatui path\n- post-migration metrics from ftui path\n- comparison report with interpretation and hotspots\n\n## Acceptance Criteria\n- metrics are reproducible and versioned\n- performance deltas are clearly explained.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:09:04.093410353Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-08T20:23:07.060157636Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ftui","perf"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-290k","depends_on_id":"wa-1hbj","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:23:07.060063160Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-290k","depends_on_id":"wa-1kut","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:09:04.129611298Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"}]}
 {"id":"wa-2dlw","title":"FTUI-01.1 Author migration ADR set (principles, scope, constraints, tradeoffs)","description":"## Background\nWe need explicit architectural decisions grounded in frankentui guidance (one-writer, deterministic pipeline, inline-first) before code migration.\n\n## Deliverables\n- ADR set defining migration intent, boundaries, and non-goals\n- explicit rationale for adopting ftui over incremental ratatui patching\n- documented tradeoffs (risk, complexity, rollout)\n\n## Acceptance Criteria\n- ADRs are reviewed and linked from the migration epic\n- every migration task references at least one accepted ADR where relevant.","notes":"Claimed after FTUI bead graph expansion; next execution step is authoring migration ADR set from frankentui principles.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"LavenderGrove","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:07:30.177530346Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-09T00:52:24.740693584Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T00:46:43.608570721Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["adr","ftui"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-2dlw","depends_on_id":"wa-p85q","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:07:30.187129462Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"}],"comments":[{"id":283,"issue_id":"wa-2dlw","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"LavenderGrove: Created ADR set in docs/adr/. @@ -187,7 +187,7 @@
 {"id":"wa-37x.5","title":"Graceful degradation modes: continue operating with reduced functionality","description":"
# Graceful Degradation Modes

## Purpose
When components fail, continue operating with reduced functionality rather than crashing.

## Degradation Scenarios

### 1. DB Read-Only Mode
**Trigger**: DB write failures (corruption, disk full)
**Behavior**:
- Continue observing panes
- Queue writes in memory (bounded)
- Log warning about degraded mode
- Resume normal operation when DB recovers

### 2. Pattern Engine Failure
**Trigger**: Pattern compilation error, regex timeout
**Behavior**:
- Skip pattern detection
- Continue ingesting output
- Log which patterns are disabled
- Partial detection if some patterns work

### 3. Workflow Engine Pause
**Trigger**: Workflow step fails repeatedly
**Behavior**:
- Pause the failing workflow
- Continue other workflows
- Keep observing for new events
- Notify user of paused workflow

### 4. WezTerm Unavailable
**Trigger**: CLI consistently fails
**Behavior**:
- Stop action attempts
- Continue any cached observation
- Poll for WezTerm availability
- Resume automatically when available

## Status Reporting
```
$ wa status

System Status: DEGRADED

Active Degradations:
  ⚠ DB writes queued (disk full) - 47 pending
  ⚠ Workflow paused: handle_usage_limits (step 3 failed)

Core Functions:
  ✓ Observation: active
  ✓ Detection: active
  ✗ Actions: degraded
```

## Recovery
- Automatic: Poll for recovery conditions
- Manual: `wa recover <component>` to retry

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Each degradation mode implemented
- [ ] Status reports degraded state clearly
- [ ] Automatic recovery when conditions clear
- [ ] Manual recovery command works
- [ ] Core observation continues in all modes

## Testing
- Unit tests for breaker/backoff logic and state transitions.
- Integration tests simulating IO failures and recovery paths.
- Chaos harness scenarios with verbose logs and recovery assertions.

 {"id":"wa-38vw","title":"[EPIC] FTUI-05 Screen-by-Screen View Migration","description":"## Purpose
Migrate all wa TUI screens/views to ftui widgets/runtime without losing operator functionality.

## Why
Screen parity is the user-visible definition of migration success.

## Focus
- navigation shell
- home/panes/events/triage/history/search/help migrations
- per-view parity matrices and behavior acceptance criteria","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:07:17.849837202Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-08T20:26:18.880261223Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ftui","migration","views"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-38vw","depends_on_id":"wa-1brb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:14:57.267645490Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-38vw","depends_on_id":"wa-1ssn","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:14:59.579669050Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-38vw","depends_on_id":"wa-2wed","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:07:17.875728911Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"}],"comments":[{"id":272,"issue_id":"wa-38vw","author":"GrayHarbor","text":"### Execution Rationale (FTUI-05)
FTUI-05 performs screen migration in a controlled order (shell -> core views -> support views) with parity ledgers per surface.","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:26:18Z"}]}
 {"id":"wa-3akp","title":"FTUI-05.4.a Events view annotation/label/triage visibility parity checklist","description":"## Background
Events view drives operator incident response; migration must preserve annotation/label/triage visibility semantics.

## Deliverables
- parity checklist for feed ordering, annotation context, labels, triage state, and redaction-sensitive fields
- validation scenarios for high-volume event streams and mixed-severity events
- artifact mapping to snapshots, adapter fixtures, and E2E transcripts

## Acceptance Criteria
- event semantics match legacy expectations or are documented as intentional deltas
- redaction behavior is explicitly verified and logged
- outputs include deterministic evidence usable in CI and post-failure diagnosis.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:14:21.138965449Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-08T21:30:43.580388678Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ftui","subtask","views"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-3akp","depends_on_id":"wa-23bz","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:14:21.158113462Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-3akp","depends_on_id":"wa-2i6m","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:25:09.608641325Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"}]}

 {"id":"wa-3cso","title":"FTUI-03.2 Enforce one-writer output routing for UI and log surfaces","description":"## Background\nfrankentui requires a one-writer rule. wa must eliminate direct stdout/stderr writes from active UI runtime paths.\n\n## Deliverables\n- unified output sink routing UI redraw + logs\n- explicit policy for subprocess output capture/routing\n- violation detection strategy in tests/CI\n\n## Acceptance Criteria\n- no direct terminal writes bypass output gate in migrated paths\n- sustained output does not corrupt cursor/layout state.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"LavenderGrove","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:07:51.521424086Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-09T01:44:58.629048059Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T01:44:58.628915122Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ftui","one-writer"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-3cso","depends_on_id":"wa-1brb","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:07:51.534554022Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-3cso","depends_on_id":"wa-2qyt","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:16:24.231283380Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"}]}
 {"id":"wa-3fed","title":"FTUI-03.4.a Add panic/abort teardown harness and restoration assertions","description":"## Background
Panic/abort paths must restore terminal usability and preserve forensic context.

## Deliverables
- teardown harness that simulates panic/abort and validates restoration invariants
- assertions for cursor mode, alt-screen state, and output routing cleanup
- failure artifact capture requirements (logs, transcripts, environment markers)

## Acceptance Criteria
- harness reproduces abrupt-exit scenarios deterministically
- restoration invariants are verified and regressions fail loudly in CI
- produced artifacts are sufficient for post-mortem without rerunning interactively.,","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:14:11.354154882Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-08T21:31:33.838982036Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ftui","lifecycle","subtask"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-3fed","depends_on_id":"wa-1p3f","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:14:11.370228861Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-3fed","depends_on_id":"wa-3gsu","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:24:45.362884125Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"}]}
 {"id":"wa-3ftn","title":"FTUI-06.3 Implement modal interactions for approvals/confirmations/errors","description":"## Background\nSafety-critical flows need explicit modal UX semantics.\n\n## Deliverables\n- reusable modal patterns (confirm/error/info) in ftui\n- keyboard-first interaction model\n- integration into existing approval/mute/critical-action flows\n\n## Acceptance Criteria\n- modal interactions are consistent and accessible\n- unsafe actions require explicit confirmation paths.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:08:28.506264195Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-08T20:19:49.770714606Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ftui","modal"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-3ftn","depends_on_id":"wa-2h1j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:19:49.770658412Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-3ftn","depends_on_id":"wa-2zd7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:08:28.515943600Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"}]}
@@ -377,7 +377,7 @@
 {"id":"wa-c7o","title":"[EPIC] Phase 2: Workflow Engine & Automation","description":"# Phase 2: Workflow Engine & Automation

## Overview
This epic builds the workflow automation system that responds to detected patterns. The workflow engine is what makes wa "active" rather than just a passive monitor - it can take corrective actions when agents hit limits, experience compaction, need authentication, etc.

## Strategic Importance
The workflow system transforms wa from a "monitoring tool" into an "automation platform". This is where the real value emerges:
- Automatic context recovery after compaction
- Automatic account rotation on usage limits
- Automatic authentication renewal
- Foundation for more sophisticated multi-agent coordination

## Key Components

### 1. Workflow Engine Architecture
- Trait-based workflow definition: \`Workflow\` trait with name, description, handles, steps, execute_step
- Step results: Continue, Done, Retry, Abort, WaitFor
- WaitCondition: Pattern, PaneIdle, External
- Per-pane workflow locking (prevent conflicting workflows)
- Idempotent, recoverable, audited execution

### 2. Handle Usage Limits Workflow (Codex First)
Steps:
1. Exit current session gracefully (Ctrl-C twice for Codex)
2. Parse session info for resume (session ID, token usage)
3. Select next available account
4. Perform account authentication
5. Resume previous session
6. Send continue prompt

This is the most complex workflow and proves out the architecture.

### 3. Handle Compaction Workflow
Steps:
1. Wait for compaction to complete (stabilization window)
2. Send agent-specific context refresh prompt
   - Claude Code: "Reread AGENTS.md so it's still fresh in your mind."
   - Codex: "Please re-read AGENTS.md and any key project context files."
   - Gemini: "Please re-examine AGENTS.md and project context."

### 4. Browser Automation Skeleton
- Playwright integration structure
- Browser profile persistence
- OpenAI device auth flow (skeleton)
- Anthropic OAuth flow (skeleton)
- Google auth flow (skeleton)
- Semi-automated fallback (open browser for human when automation fails)

### 5. CAUT Integration
- Shell out to \`caut\` for usage tracking
- get_usage(service) - current usage for OpenAI/Anthropic/Google
- refresh_usage(service) - refresh from APIs
- Account selection logic based on percent_remaining

## Workflow Design Constraints
Every workflow must be:
- **Idempotent**: Re-running doesn't make a mess
- **Recoverable**: Can resume after a crash
- **Audited**: Every step recorded (what was observed, what was sent)
- **Guarded**: Each step validates state before action

## Workflow Execution Model
Each step follows: Observe → Decide → Act → Verify

Step can return:
- \`Continue\` - proceed to next step
- \`WaitFor(condition)\` - pause until condition met
- \`Retry(after)\` - retry same step after delay
- \`Abort(reason)\` - stop workflow with error
- \`Done(result)\` - workflow completed successfully

## Data-Driven Workflow Descriptors
In addition to compiled Rust workflows, support YAML/TOML descriptors for simple prompt injection sequences:

\`\`\`yaml
# ~/.config/wa/workflows/custom_compaction.yaml
name: custom_compaction_prompt
trigger:
  event: session.compaction
  agent: claude_code
steps:
  - wait_stable: 2s
  - send: |
      Please re-read AGENTS.md and the project's README.md.
  - wait_for: "❯"
\`\`\`

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Workflow engine can execute multi-step workflows
- [ ] handle_compaction workflow works end-to-end for Claude Code
- [ ] handle_usage_limits workflow works end-to-end for Codex (at least skeleton)
- [ ] Browser automation structure is in place (can be extended in Phase 3)
- [ ] CAUT integration returns account usage data
- [ ] Workflows are audited and recoverable

## Dependencies
- Depends on Phase 1 (Foundation) - needs pattern engine, storage, watcher

## Technical Notes

### Why Codex First for Usage Limits?
- Codex has the clearest device auth flow
- Resume mechanism is well-documented
- Good test case for the complexity

### Semi-Automated Browser Auth
If Playwright can't complete auth (MFA, CAPTCHA, password):
1. Open non-headless browser
2. Request human to complete auth
3. Persist browser profile for future sessions
4. Notify when auth complete

This provides graceful degradation.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T08:47:54.895212760Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T08:55:07.794108825Z","closed_at":"2026-01-18T08:55:07.794108825Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of wa-nu4.1 (Phase 2: Workflows). wa-nu4.1 has correct dependency chain. Content merged conceptually.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"wa-cjn","title":"E2E test scripts with detailed logging","description":"## Scripts to Create

- Start watcher against mock pane
- Verify segment capture
- Test gap detection

### scripts/test_patterns.sh
- Run pattern engine against corpus
- Verify all detections match expected

## Logging Requirements
All scripts must:
1. Print "[TIMESTAMP] [TEST_NAME] Starting..."
2. Print "[TIMESTAMP] [TEST_NAME] PASS/FAIL: reason"
3. Save full logs to /tmp/wa_e2e_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS.log
4. Support --verbose for extra output
5. Exit 0 if all pass, 1+ if any fail

- `./scripts/e2e_test.sh` runs without error
- Clear pass/fail output

 {"id":"wa-dhd","title":"Structured logging infrastructure: JSON format, tracing spans, contextual fields","description":"# Structured Logging Infrastructure

## Purpose
Implement consistent structured logging across all wa components.

## Implementation

### Tracing Setup
```rust
use tracing_subscriber::{fmt, EnvFilter, layer::SubscriberExt};

pub fn init_logging(config: &LogConfig) -> Result<()> {
    let filter = EnvFilter::try_from_default_env()
        .unwrap_or_else(|_| EnvFilter::new(&config.log_level));
    
    let subscriber = tracing_subscriber::registry()
        .with(filter)
        .with(match config.log_format {
            LogFormat::Json => fmt::layer().json().boxed(),
            LogFormat::Pretty => fmt::layer().pretty().boxed(),
        });
    
    tracing::subscriber::set_global_default(subscriber)?;
    Ok(())
}
```

### Contextual Spans
```rust
#[tracing::instrument(
    skip(self, text),
    fields(pane_id = %pane_id, text_len = text.len())
)]
pub async fn send_text(&self, pane_id: u32, text: &str) -> Result<()> {
    // ... implementation
}
```

### Log Entry Structure
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-01-18T14:30:00.123Z",
  "level": "INFO",
  "target": "wa::ingest",
  "message": "Segment persisted",
  "pane_id": 3,
  "segment_id": 12345,
  "bytes": 1024,
  "duration_ms": 2.5
}
```

### Sensitive Data Redaction
```rust
impl Redactable for SendTextAction {
    fn redact(&self) -> Self {
        Self {
            text: redact_secrets(&self.text),
            ..self.clone()
        }
    }
}

fn redact_secrets(text: &str) -> String {
    // Patterns: API keys, tokens, passwords
    REDACTION_PATTERNS.iter()
        .fold(text.to_string(), |s, (pattern, replacement)| {
            pattern.replace_all(&s, *replacement).to_string()
        })
}
```

## Log Levels
- **trace**: Detailed internal operations (segment boundaries, pattern checks)
- **debug**: Diagnostic information (config loaded, connection established)
- **info**: Normal operations (workflow started, event detected)
- **warn**: Recoverable issues (retry needed, fallback used)
- **error**: Failures requiring attention (workflow failed, connection lost)

## Testing
- Unit: JSON format is valid, redaction works
- Integration: Log levels filter correctly
- E2E: Logs appear in expected format during operations

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] tracing subscriber configured with JSON and pretty modes
- [ ] All public functions have appropriate spans
- [ ] Contextual fields (pane_id, workflow_id) propagate
- [ ] Sensitive data redacted from logs
- [ ] Log levels documented and consistent

 {"id":"wa-dug","title":"[EPIC] Smart Environment Detection: auto-configure based on detected setup","description":"# [EPIC] Smart Environment Detection

## Mission
Automatically detect the user's environment and configure wa optimally, reducing setup friction.

## Why This Matters
Users struggle with configuration:
- "Which settings do I need?"
- "Why isn't it detecting my agents?"
- "What's the right poll interval?"

Smart detection provides:
- **Zero-config start**: Works out of the box
- **Optimal defaults**: Tuned for detected environment
- **Guided setup**: Suggests improvements

## Scope

### Environment Detection
- WezTerm version and capabilities
- Shell type (bash, zsh, fish)
- OSC 133 support
- Agent presence and types
- Remote panes (SSH)
- System resources (CPU, RAM)

### Auto-configuration
- Poll intervals based on system load
- Pattern packs based on detected agents
- Storage paths based on OS conventions
- Safety defaults based on environment

### Detection API
```rust
pub struct DetectedEnvironment {
    wezterm: WeztermInfo,
    shell: ShellInfo,
    osc_133_support: bool,
    agents: Vec<DetectedAgent>,
    remotes: Vec<RemoteHost>,
    system: SystemInfo,
}
```

### CLI Integration
- `wa doctor` shows detection results
- `wa setup` uses detection for suggestions
- Config shows effective settings with sources

## Success Criteria
- New users need no configuration to start
- Detection is accurate for common setups
- Suggestions improve user experience

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Environment detection API complete
- [ ] Auto-configuration uses detection
- [ ] wa doctor shows detection results
- [ ] Suggestions are actionable
- [ ] Tests cover detection scenarios

## Testing
- Unit tests for probes and recommendation rules.
- Integration tests for doctor/setup output.
- E2E artifacts include detection results and rationale.

 {"id":"wa-dug.1","title":"Environment detection probes + API","description":"# Task: Environment detection probes + API

## Goal
Implement the **detected environment** API and probes used by setup/doctor/suggestions.

## Scope
- WezTerm detection:
  - version
  - mux server presence
  - pane count
- Shell detection:
  - bash/zsh/fish
  - OSC 133 support
- Agent detection:
  - known agent banners in recent output
- System resources:
  - CPU count
  - memory estimate

## Deliverables
- `DetectedEnvironment` struct + serialization
- Probe functions with bounded timeouts
- Cached detection results (avoid repeated slow checks)

## Testing
- Unit tests with mocked probes
- Integration tests with fixture outputs (wezterm list, shell markers)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Detection API returns consistent results and never blocks CLI for long.
- All probes have timeouts and actionable error messages.
